summary: The mystical, ascetic dimension of Islam, organized into orders called tariqa (plural turuq) that trace teaching lineages (silsila) back to Muhammad. Best known in the West for the "whirling dervishes" of the Mevlevi order, founded by followers of the poet Rumi, and for the devotional qawwali music popularized by Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan. Core practices center on dhikr (ritual remembrance/recitation of God's names) and the goal of achieving tawhid, divine unity, often through ego-annihilation (fana). Major orders include the Naqshbandi, Qadiriyya, Chishti, Tijaniyya, and the West African Muridiyya. Foundational thinkers include the poet-mystics Rumi and Ibn Arabi and the theologian al-Ghazali, who reconciled Sufism with mainstream Sunni orthodoxy.

works.name: Dhikr
works.description: The central Sufi practice of "remembrance" — ritualized recitation or chanting, often of Allah's 99 names or short devotional phrases, sometimes counted on prayer beads (misbaha/tasbih) or synchronized with the heartbeat. Can be performed silently (notably in the Naqshbandi order) or aloud, and may be accompanied by music, dance, or the whirling of the Mevlevi order, which is itself considered a form of dhikr.

clues.clue: Name means "remembrance"; involves chanting/reciting phrases (often the 99 names of God) to focus on or contemplate God, sometimes using prayer beads
examples: Many followers of this tradition engage in highly ritualized forms of dhikr, a form of meditation or repetition of words to aid in contemplating God.
This form of meditative practice, whose name means "remembrance," involves chanting phrases in order to focus on God.

clues.clue: The Mevlevi order's whirling is itself considered a form of dhikr
examples: The whirling of the Mevlevi order is a form of this practice.
Dervishes whirl while performing this action of remembering God or verses from the Qu'ran or Hadith.

clues.clue: The Naqshbandi order practices a distinctive silent form, sometimes synchronizing invocations of God's names with the heartbeat
examples: Members of this tradition seek to become closer to Allah by practicing various types of dhikr, including a silent form practiced by the Naqshbandi order.
This lineage's Naqshbandi school teaches a practice where one synchronizes invocations of God's names with heartbeats.

clues.clue: Per Surah Ar-Ra'd, Sufis' hearts "find peace" through this practice of chanting du'a and Allah's many names
examples: Per Surah Ar-Ra'd, many Sufis' hearts "find peace" when they engage in this practice of remembrance by chanting various du'a and Allah's many names.

clues.clue: Institutionalizing group dhikr through the sama ceremony has been derided by critics as bid'ah ("innovation")
examples: This faith's sama ceremony is often derided as bid'ah for institutionalizing the group practice of dhikr, the recitation of the names of God.

works.name: Sama and the Mevlevi Whirling Ceremony
works.description: The devotional ceremony (also called sema) most associated with the Mevlevi order, founded by followers of Rumi and known in the West as the "Whirling Dervishes." Practitioners spin in a section called the Four Salams, often accompanied by the ney (reed flute) and an improvisatory solo called a taksim, while wearing garb including the tannur frock, destegul, sikke cap, and colored tanoura garment. The goal is wajd, ecstatic union with God; some traditions link the turning to the movement of the planets.

clues.clue: Ceremony (sama/sema) features the ney flute; may include a taksim (improvisatory flute solo) and tambourines
examples: Sama or sema ceremonies among this tradition often feature the ney flute and tambourines.
A ritual from a school of this tradition involves a flute improvisation called taksim and is called sama.

clues.clue: Whirling/spinning dance performed by dervishes, possibly inspired by the movement of the planets around the sun; occurs specifically during the ceremony's "Four Salams" section
examples: Mevlevi sama ceremonies often include this ritual action, which is performed by "dervishes" and may have been inspired by the movement of the planets around the sun.
Whirling only occurs during this section of the Sama ceremony. This section's name comes from the number of times the believers welcome Allah during the spiritual journey.

clues.clue: Goal of wajd (ecstasy); some adherents use erotic poetry toward this end
examples: Some adherents of this tradition may use erotic poetry in the sama ceremony for the goal of attaining wajd, or ecstasy.

clues.clue: Ceremonial garb includes the white frock tannur, long-sleeved destegul, pill-shaped sikke cap; different branches represented by colors of the tanoura garment
examples: A devotional practice among this group involves clothing like the white frock tannur and the long-sleeved destegul, as well as a tall, somewhat pill-shaped cap called a sikke.
Different branches of this tradition are represented by each of the bright colors of the tanoura garment.

clues.clue: The Mevlevi order, best known in the West, was founded by followers of Rumi and is known as the "Whirling Dervishes"
examples: This Sufi Order was founded by the followers of Sufi Mystic Rumi. You can give either the official name or the name they are known by from their famous dance.
For 10 points, name this group whose Mevlevi order is known as the "Whirling Dervishes."

works.name: Qawwali and Sufi Devotional Music
works.description: A South Asian devotional song style, historically traced to the Chishti order, sung by qawwals such as Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan (nicknamed "Shahenshah"/"King of Kings" of qawwali) and the Sabri Brothers, often performed at urs ceremonies (death-anniversary festivals at Sufi shrines). Nusrat's crossover work — the album Mustt Mustt (with Michael Brook), collaborations with Peter Gabriel, and a Massive Attack remix — carried Sufi music to Western audiences. Distinct regional Sufi music traditions include Moroccan Sufi ensembles (Master Musicians of Joujouka) and West African/Sahelian traditions studied for their influence on gnawa music.

clues.clue: Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan, "Shahenshah"/"King of Kings" of qawwali, performed vocals in this devotional South Asian genre
examples: This religious tradition's devotional singers include Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan, a qawwali performer.
Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan was known as the "Shahenshah," or "king of kings," of qawwali, a devotional music style of this religious tradition.

clues.clue: Nusrat's crossover album Mustt Mustt (with Michael Brook), Peter Gabriel's Last Temptation of Christ soundtrack, and a Massive Attack remix of "Mustt Mustt" (the first Urdu song to chart in the UK) brought this music to Western audiences
examples: A member of this tradition collaborated with Michael Brook on Night Song and released the album Mustt Mustt.
The first Urdu song to chart in the UK was this band's electronic remix of Nusrat's song "Mustt Mustt."

clues.clue: The Sabri Brothers performed this song style, commonly played at urs ceremonies
examples: A style of music from this religious tradition commonly played at urs ceremonies is performed by The Sabri Brothers.
Performing a song form associated with this tradition brought fame to the Sabri Brothers.

clues.clue: The Chishti order gave rise to qawwali singing in South Asia
examples: A group from this tradition called the Chishtis gave rise to Qawwali singing in South Asia.

clues.clue: Moroccan Sufi ensembles like the Master Musicians of Joujouka play instruments such as the lira and gimbri; this music strongly influenced gnawa music
examples: At an annual festival limited to 50 attendees, musicians from this religious tradition play instruments like the lira and gimbri.
That style from this tradition strongly influenced gnawa music.

clues.clue: Richard C. Jankowsky studied this tradition's "ambient" musical character, examining contributions from women, Jews, and laborers
examples: The contributions of women, Jews, and hard-drinking laborers to this tradition's music are examined in a Richard C. Jankowsky study of its "ambient" character.

works.name: Sufi Orders (Turuq)
works.description: Organized schools/brotherhoods (tariqa, plural turuq) through which most Sufis practice, each led by a murshid (guide) whose disciples (muridun) trace a spiritual lineage (silsila) back to Muhammad. Members may receive a ceremonial cloak (khirqa) and gather at lodges called khanqah, tekke, or zawiya. Beyond the Mevlevi (covered separately), major orders include the Naqshbandi (tracing its lineage through Abu Bakr rather than Ali, founded by Baha' al-Din Naqshband of Bukhara, and including a reformist Mujaddidi offshoot founded by Ahmad Sirhindi and spread by Khalid al-Baghdadi), the Qadiriyya, the Chishti Order (brought to India by a saint buried at Ajmer; patronized by Akbar and including the musician Amir Khusrau), the Tijaniyya (founded by Ahmad al-Tijani, one of the first to require exclusive membership), and the Senussi (descended from Idris I). Fazlur Rahman theorized a more politically active "neo-Sufism" emerging in the orders' 18th-19th century history.

clues.clue: General organizational structure: disciples (muridun) follow a guide (murshid); orders trace lineages (silsila) back to Muhammad or Ali; members may receive a cloak (khirqa) and gather at lodges (khanqah/tekke/zawiya)
examples: Followers of this tradition have a "silsila" forming a chain of teachers back to Muhammad, and may be organized into a "tariqa" or order.

Those joining these organizations often receive a cloak called a khirqa and many formed around meeting places called a khanqah or tekke, and leaders of them often trace spiritual lineages called silsila to Ali.

clues.clue: Major named orders (turuq) recur across clues as a group: Naqshbandi, Qadiriyya, Chishti, Bektashi, Mevlevi, Tijaniyya, Senussi
examples: Orders in this tradition called tariqat include the Chishtis, Qadiris, and Naqshbandis.
Name this tradition whose tariqa lineages include the Bektashi and Naqshbandi orders.

clues.clue: Naqshbandi order: unusually traces its teaching lineage through the first Caliph Abu Bakr (rather than Ali); founded/named for Baha' al-Din Naqshband of Bukhara
examples: Unlike other Sufi turuq, the Naqshbandi school traces its teachings back through this first Caliph, a father-in-law of Muhammad.
Baha' al-Din Naqshband, the namesake of Naqshbandi Sufism, was from this city where the Samanids had been based.

clues.clue: A Naqshbandi offshoot arose from debate between "unity of witness" and "unity of being," founded by Ahmad Sirhindi and spread to Iraq by Khalid al-Baghdadi
examples: Debates between the positions of "unity of witness" and "unity of being" resulted in an offshoot of one of these groups in India that was spread to Iraq by Khalid al-Baghdadi and that was founded by Ahmad Sirhindi.

clues.clue: Chishti Order: brought to India by a saint buried at a famous dargah in Ajmer; patron included Akbar the Great; members included the musician Amir Khusrau and Salim Chishti (mentor of Akbar's son, buried in a marble tomb at Fatehpur Sikri)
examples: Akbar the Great was a patron of this Sufi order, to which the musician Amir Khusrau belonged. The Sufi saint who brought this order to India is buried in a famous dargah at Ajmer.

One of this religion's saints was rewarded for service at the Battle of Haldighati with a marble tomb that became the center of Fatehpur Sikri; that mentor of Akbar, Salim, was a member of this faith's Chishti Order.

clues.clue: Tijaniyya order founded by Ahmad al-Tijani, one of the first to require exclusive membership
examples: Ahmad al-Tijani founded one of the first of them to require exclusive membership.

clues.clue: Senussi order descends from Idris I; based in North Africa alongside the Turkish Mevlevi
examples: Idris I descended from the founder of its Senussi order, and it also includes the Turkish Mevlevi.

clues.clue: Fazlur Rahman theorized a more politically active "neo-Sufism" arising in orders during the 18th-19th centuries, at the cost of their "metaphysical character"
examples: Fazlur Rahman argued that these organizations started to emphasize political activism in the 18th and 19th centuries at the cost of their "metaphysical character" in his hypothesis of their "neo-" version.

works.name: Rumi
works.description: 13th-century Persian poet whose teachings inspired the Mevlevi order. Author of the Masnavi ("Spiritual Couplets"), which opens with the "Song of the Reed Flute," and the Divan-i Shams-i Tabrizi, a collection of ninety ghazals dedicated to his spiritual teacher Shams-i Tabrizi. His poetry was popularized in English by Coleman Barks's loose translations, criticized for stripping out Islamic content; he is also the subject of the novel The Forty Rules of Love.

clues.clue: Wrote the Masnavi ("Spiritual Couplets"), which inspired the Mevlevi Order
examples: A 13th century poet who wrote the Spiritual Couplets inspired the Mevlevi Order of this tradition, which includes whirling dervishes.
That follower of this religion wrote the Spiritual Couplets.

clues.clue: Wrote "The Song of the Reed Flute"; close relationship with his spiritual teacher/instructor Shams-i-Tabrizi/Shams of Tabriz
examples: Name this author, who wrote "The Song of the Reed Flute". A collection by this author depicts their closeness with their spiritual instructor Shams-i-Tabrizi.
Poems that were loosely translated into English by Coleman Barks were written by an adherent of this religion who wrote the "Song of the Reed Flute."

clues.clue: Collected ninety ghazals in his Divan, dedicated to Shams of Tabriz; also wrote a collection of discourses called "It Is What It Is"
examples: A follower of this religion collected ninety ghazals in his Divan, which was dedicated to his teacher, Shams of Tabriz.
A book of discourses called "It Is What It Is" was collected by an adherent of this religion.

clues.clue: Translator Coleman Barks (The Essential Rumi) has been criticized for erasing Islamic elements from Rumi's poetry despite not reading or speaking Persian
examples: This translator of Rumi has been criticised for erasing Islamic elements from Rumi's poetry. This American academic neither reads nor speaks Persian, but wrote the so-called translation The Essential Rumi.

clues.clue: Subject of the novel The Forty Rules of Love, in which every chapter starts with the letter "B"
examples: A novel about an adherent of this religion, in which every chapter starts with the letter "B," is titled The Forty Rules of Love.

works.name: Ibn Arabi
works.description: 13th-century Andalusian scholar and mystic, called ash-Shaykh al-Akbar ("the Greatest Master") for his foundational influence on Sufism despite not identifying with the tradition himself. Author of The Meccan Openings/Illuminations and The Ringstones/Bezels of Wisdom, in which he developed the concept of al-insan al-kamil ("the Perfect Man," compared to a polished mirror) and wahdat al-wujud ("the unity of being"), taking tawhid — not existence — as the ultimate truth. Also used the concept of barzakh to describe how things simultaneously unite and divide, and collected erotic poetry (addressed to doves) in The Interpreter of Desires.

clues.clue: Andalusian scholar and mystic, author of The Ringstones/Bezels of Wisdom and The Meccan Openings/Illuminations; called ash-Shaykh al-Akbar ("the Greatest Master") though he did not consider himself part of Sufism
examples: Name this Andalusian scholar and mystic, the author of The Ringstones of Wisdom. His heavily Qur'an-focused work devotes much attention to the implications of God's "engendering command," or amr, to "Be!"

While Ibn Arabi did not consider himself part of this broad Islamic mystical tradition, his work was so foundational to its various branches that he is called ash-Shaykh al-Abkar, or its greatest shaykh.

clues.clue: Developed al-insan al-kamil, the "Perfect Man" compared to a "polished mirror" who understands the unity (wahdat al-wujud) of being/existence
examples: Identify this attribute of a "man" who is compared to a "polished mirror" in the mystical philosophy of Ibn 'Arabi.
Ibn 'Arabi's "perfect man" understands the unity, or wahdat, of this concept, whose "univocity" was theorized by Duns Scotus.

clues.clue: Held tawhid — God's oneness/singularity, not existence — to be the ultimate truth underlying all other truths
examples: It's not existence, but Ibn 'Arabi took this property of God, known as tawhid in Arabic, to be the truth upon which all other truths in his philosophy are based.
According to ibn Arabi, once one reaches the second level of yaqin, they truly understand this aspect of Allah, his "oneness" and "singularity."

clues.clue: Used the concept of barzakh to describe how things simultaneously unite and divide
examples: In discussing the role of imagination, he used the concept of barzakh to describe how things simultaneously unite and divide.

clues.clue: Collected erotic poems (nasib form) addressed to doves in The Interpreter of Desires
examples: A poem in ibn Arabi's The Interpreter of Desires addresses the command "gentle now" to these animals "of the thornberry and moringa thicket."

clues.clue: Toshihiko Izutsu linked Ibn Arabi's "perfect man" concept to the sages of Daoism in a comparative book
examples: Toshihiko Izutsu linked the "perfect man" with this tradition's sages in a book titled for "Sufism and" this tradition.

works.name: Al-Ghazali
works.description: Islamic theologian of the Golden Age whose Revival of the Religious Sciences (Ihya) made Sufism acceptable within mainstream Sunni orthodoxy by defending it as directly inspired by the Quran. Also wrote The Incoherence of the Philosophers, a devastating critique of the Aristotelian falsafa tradition of al-Farabi, Avicenna, and Averroes (who wrote a rebuttal, The Incoherence of the Incoherence), and promoted occasionalism. His commentary The Niche of Lights discusses the Quran's Light Verse and three types of light, concluding God is the only true light.

clues.clue: Revival of the Religious Sciences (Ihya) made Sufism acceptable/mainstream within orthodox Sunni Islam, defending it as inspired by the Quran
examples: This Muslim theologian's The Revival of the Religious Sciences made Sufism acceptable in Orthodox Islam.
In The Revival of Religious Sciences, al-Ghazali defends this mystical Islamic tradition of which he was a part as being directly inspired by the Koran.

clues.clue: Wrote The Incoherence of the Philosophers, a critique of Aristotelianism/falsafa (al-Farabi, Avicenna, Averroes) and of ijtihad, refuted by Averroes
examples: Aristotelianism is incompatible with this religion, according to a book refuted by Averroes called The Incoherence of the Philosophers.
This man attacked ijtihad, particularly as used by Avicenna. He believed overconfidence in personal judgments were responsible for the errors he listed in his The Incoherence of the Philosophers.

clues.clue: The Niche of Lights, a commentary on the Quran's Light Verse, argues God is the only true light and promotes occasionalism
examples: This Islamic scholar's commentary The Niche of Lights considers three types of light and says that the only true light is God, who is pure light. He promoted occasionalism in a treatise attacking Aristotelian philosophy.

clues.clue: Influenced Usman dan Fodio's treatise supporting the teaching of religious sciences in the Sokoto Caliphate
examples: Usman Dan Fodio's treatise on the revival of the traditions and support for the teaching of religious sciences was influenced by this Islamic theologian's Revival of the Religious Sciences.

works.name: Fana, Baqa, and Tawhid
works.description: The core mystical goal-concepts of Sufi practice: fana, the annihilation of the ego/self (nafs), summarized as "dying before one dies"; baqa, the resulting state of "perpetual existence" or alignment with the divine plan; and tawhid, the ultimate divine unity that practices like dhikr and sama aim to help the practitioner realize. Advanced practitioners may seek to unlock supersensory organs called the Lataif-e-sitta ("Six Subtleties"), and yaqin ("certainty," a form of gnosis) describes degrees of mystical knowing.

clues.clue: Fana (ego-annihilation, "dying before one dies") is followed by baqa ("perpetual existence"), a three-graded state of alignment with the divine plan; the struggle against the nafs is a form of the "greater jihad"
examples: The concept of fana' is followed by baqa', or "perpetual existence," in this tradition whose practices often revolve around dhikr, or remembrance of God.
This lineage developed a concept of ego-annihilation termed "dying before one dies," enabling a three-graded state of alignment with the divine plan.

clues.clue: Ultimate goal is achieving tawhid, divine unity, popularized/pursued through practices like Rumi's poetry and the sama ceremony
examples: Achieving the ultimate divine unity embodied by tawhid is the goal of this faith's most famous practice, which was popularized by poets like Rumi.

clues.clue: Some adherents unlock supersensory organs called Lataif-e-sitta ("The Six Subtleties") as part of spiritual development
examples: Some adherents of this religious practice are trained to unlock within themselves a group of super-sensory organs known as Lataif-e-sitta, or "The Six Subtleties."
And six suprasensory organs called the lataif-e-sitta are distinguished by this sect.

clues.clue: Yaqin ("certainty," a form of gnosis) describes graded degrees of mystical knowing/trance
examples: Yaqin is a form of gnosis, or knowing, in this branch of mystical Islam. Its members, the whirling dervishes, often enter a trance-like state through spinning.

clues.clue: Members are sometimes called "desirous ones"; developed the idea of Insan-i Kamil ("Perfected Man") heading a hierarchy of saints who uphold world order
examples: Members of this religious tradition are sometimes referred to using a term that literally translates to "desirous ones." Followers of this tradition originated the idea of the Insan-i Kamil, or the "Perfected Man" who heads a hierarchy of saints who uphold the world order.

works.name: West African Sufism: Sokoto Caliphate and the Muridiyya
works.description: West African Sufism centers on two major movements: the Qadiriyya-affiliated Sokoto Caliphate, founded by Usman dan Fodio (whose daughter, the scholar-poet Nana Asma'u, founded the Yan Taru women's education movement and wrote poetry praising Sufi women including the saint Rabi'a Basri); and the Muridiyya ("Mouride") brotherhood, based in Touba, Senegal and founded by Amadou Bamba, whose massive annual Grand Magal pilgrimage draws adherents who drink coffee flavored with djar seeds. Politically influential in Senegal, the Muridiyya was also noted by pan-Africanist theorist Cheikh Anta Diop, a native of the same region.

clues.clue: Sokoto Caliphate: Fulani Islamic state founded by Usman dan Fodio, who took the Fulani people on jihad and conquered Hausaland; follows the Qadiriyya order
examples: Name this West African Islamic state founded by Nana Asma'u's father, Usman Dan Fodio, who sought to spread religious truth.
Usman dan Fodio and Nana Asma'u belonged to the Qadiriyya order of this mystical Islamic tradition.

clues.clue: Nana Asma'u, daughter of Usman dan Fodio, founded the Yan Taru movement of women's educators and wrote a poem praising Sufi women, mentioning the sainthood of Rabi'a Basri
examples: Name this scholar and poet who founded the Yan Taru movement, a network of women's educators who traveled to rural communities to teach Islamic theology.
A poem in praise of the women of this tradition, which mentions the sainthood of Rabi'a Basri, was written by Nana Asma'u.

clues.clue: Muridiyya ("Mouride") brotherhood based in Touba, Senegal; founded by/commemorates Amadou Bamba via the massive annual Grand Magal pilgrimage, during which members drink coffee flavored with djar seeds
examples: A massive annual pilgrimage among this lineage called the Grand Magal commemorates Amadou Bamba.
Members of this tradition drink coffee flavored with peppery seeds called djar during the Grand Magal pilgrimage to Touba, where this tradition's Mouride offshoot is based.

clues.clue: Muridiyya has de facto political rule over Touba in Senegal; noted by pan-Africanist theorist Cheikh Anta Diop, a native of Senegal
examples: Diop was born in this modern country, where his politically-influential Muridiyya sect has de facto rule over their holy city of Touba.

clues.clue: The Muridiyya and Tijaniyya are cited as African-based examples of Sufi "brotherhoods"
examples: The Muridiyya and Tijaniyya are African-based examples of "brotherhoods" based on this tradition of Islamic mysticism.

works.name: Shrine Pilgrimage and Holidays
works.description: Sufism places heavy emphasis on venerating saints at their tombs (dargah in South Asia, marabouts in West/North Africa, ribats-turned-shrines in the Maghreb), gathering at lodges (khanqah/tekke/zawiya), and marking holidays tied to saints' death anniversaries (urs) or the Prophet's birth (Mawlid an-Nabi). Critics like Salafis and Wahhabis accuse such practices of shirk (idolatry) or bid'ah (unlawful innovation).

clues.clue: Popular practice of visiting tombs/shrines (dargah) of favored Sufi saints; Muhammad's own tomb is marked by a green dome
examples: A popular Sufi practice is to visit these locations belonging to their favorite Sufi guru. The one belonging to Muhammad has a green dome above it.
Buildings called khanqah are constructed near dargah shrines in this tradition.

clues.clue: Maghrebi marabouts (wandering teachers, from a root meaning "garrison") are venerated at tomb-pilgrimage sites, possessing high levels of baraka; originally lived in monastic fortresses called ribats; a "Grand" marabout leads the Mouride brotherhood
examples: The tombs of these wandering African Islamic teachers, from an Arabic word root meaning "garrison," often serve as pilgrimage sites. A "Grand" one leads the Mouride brotherhood, which about 40% of Senegal belongs to.
Many Muslims in the Maghreb undertake pilgrimages to the tombs of these Sufi leaders, who originally lived in monastic fortresses called ribats.

clues.clue: Uch and Multan, Pakistan earned the nickname "City of Saints" for their preponderance of Sufi mausoleums
examples: The preponderance of mausoleums of figures from this tradition garnered Uch and Multan, Pakistan the nickname "City of Saints."

clues.clue: Mawlid an-Nabi, a carnival-esque holiday commemorating Muhammad's birth, was popularized by Sufism; Ahmadiyya and Wahhabi communities reject it as bid'ah ("innovation")
examples: Mawlid celebration was popularized by this mystical movement in Islam, which emphasizes a personal relationship with Allah.
Ahmadiyya and Wahhabi communities do not practice Mawlid, because they consider it this sort of practice.

clues.clue: Critics (Salafis and others) accuse Sufis of shirk, the sin of ascribing godlike attributes to idols/saints
examples: Salafis and others often accuse Sufis of this practice, which roughly translates as "polytheism."

clues.clue: In Harer, Ethiopia, Sufis chew qat to facilitate spiritual experiences and heighten their sense of union with Allah, and commemorate Ashura by feeding hyenas porridge
examples: In Harer, qat is mainly used by members of this mystical sect of Islam to facilitate spiritual experiences and heighten their sense of union with Allah.
Sufis in Harer commemorate this holiday by feeding hyenas porridge to receive prosperity.

clues.clue: The 40-day chilla retreat (fasting/praying in a dark space) is most commonly performed within Sufism
examples: Chilla is most commonly performed by Muslims associated with this Islamic mystical tradition, which is divided into many orders.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Standard closing/identifying clues for Sufism as a whole, plus miscellaneous biographical, etymological, and historical facts that did not cluster into a larger section.

clues.clue: Mystical dimension/tradition of Islam whose followers are known as dervishes; "Sufi" possibly derives from an Arabic root meaning "those who wear wool"
examples: For 10 points, identify this sect of mystical Islam whose followers are often known as "Dervishes" for their whirling.
People from this tradition were historically depicted wearing woolen clothes, reflecting the possible etymology of this tradition's name as referring to "those who wear wool."

clues.clue: Spread widely along Central Asian trade routes/the steppes, often accompanying merchant caravans
examples: Islam was spread throughout the steppes largely by followers of this mystical tradition who often accompanied those merchant caravans.

clues.clue: Early Western scholarship on Sufism was popularized by Ivan Agueli and Rene Guenon
examples: Early Western scholarship on this faith was popularized by Ivan Agueli and Rene Guinon, and its major schools include the Naqshbandi and Mevlevi.

clues.clue: Al-Muhasibi and his disciple Junayd are cited as early figures in the tradition
examples: Al-Muhasibi and his disciple Junayd are early figures in this tradition.

clues.clue: Practitioners are also called "fakir," originally used for mendicant dervishes
examples: Some Sufis are referred to by this term, which was originally used to describe mendicant dervishes.

clues.clue: Western offshoots/new religious movements drew on Sufism: Meher Baba (founder of Sufism Reoriented, brought to Australia by Friedrich von Frankenberg's student Francis Brabazon, who built the "Avatar's Abode"), and Inayat Khan (founder of the International Sufi Movement, who framed unity of God with all things as tawhid)
examples: This tradition was introduced to Australia by Friedrich von Frankenberg, whose student Francis Brabazon built the "Avatar's Abode" to house the leader of "[this tradition] Reoriented."
Inayat Khan, the founder of the International Sufi Movement, centered his belief on the unity of God with all things, which he referred to with this term.

clues.clue: Chinese practitioners construct gongbei memorials on the graves of menhuan lineage founders, such as the early modern leader Ma Laichi, whose shrine is in Linxia
examples: The city of Linxia is home to the shrine of an early modern Chinese leader in this religious lineage named Ma Laichi.

clues.clue: Historically influential among Central Asian dynasties: the Safavids' originally Kurdish ruling family rose to prominence as tariqa leaders before Shah Ismail I established Shi'a Islam in Iran
examples: The Safavids established Shi'a Islam as the state religion of Iran under Ismail I, whose originally Kurdish family had risen to prominence as leaders of tariqas of this mystical Islamic tradition.

clues.clue: The Darqawa order led a 19th-century revival of ascetic Sufism in Morocco
examples: The Darqawa order led a revival of ascetic Sufism in this country, which Clifford Geertz contrasted with Indonesia in Islam Observed.

clues.clue: Practiced widely by Kurds (alongside the distinct religion Yazidism) as part of a tariqa network, with particular devotion to dhikr
examples: Many Kurds follow this Islamic tradition as part of a tariqa network. Followers of this tradition are particularly devoted to dhikr, or reciting the names of God.

clues.clue: Poets Hafez and Rumi wrote love poetry within this tradition expressing desire for union with the Beloved; Mahmoud Shabestari's The Secret Rose Garden and Attar of Nishapur's The Conference of the Birds are other major mystical poems
examples: Name this mystical sect whose poets Hafez and Rumi often wrote love poems expressing desire for union with the Beloved.
Mahmoud Shabestari wrote a book of poems in this Islamic mystic tradition called The Secret Rose Garden.

clues.clue: A saint named Khwajah Abdullah Ansari criticized kalam theology in his Dhamm al-Kalam; thinkers in the tradition write about "centers of spiritual perception"
examples: A saint of this tradition named Khwajah Abdullah Ansari criticized kalam theology in his Dhamm al-Kalam.

clues.clue: The Quran's Light Verse, describing all things as created from light, is particularly popular in this tradition
examples: The Light Verse is particularly popular in this mystical Islamic tradition, which holds that all things were created from light.

clues.clue: Al-Ghazali's critique of falsafa helped bring this tradition into the mainstream, per 12th-century Islamic intellectual history
examples: To accompany his devastating critique of falsafa, al-Ghazali was one of the first Islamic theologians to bring this tradition into the mainstream.

clues.clue: Inspired the naming of Islam-inspired rock subgenres: "Sufi rock," exemplified by the Pakistani band Junoon
examples: Another Islam-inspired rock subgenre is named for this mystical movement, exemplified by the Pakistani band Junoon.

comprehensive_summary: Sufism (tasawwuf) is the mystical, ascetic dimension of Islam, organized primarily through orders called tariqa (plural turuq), each led by a spiritual guide (murshid) whose disciples (muridun) trace a teaching lineage (silsila) back to Muhammad or, in the distinctive case of the Naqshbandi order (founded by Baha' al-Din Naqshband of Bukhara), back through the first Caliph Abu Bakr. Orders gather at lodges called khanqah, tekke, or zawiya, and new members may receive a ceremonial cloak (khirqa). Beyond the Naqshbandi, major orders recurring across clues include the Qadiriyya; the Chishti Order, brought to India by a saint buried at Ajmer and patronized by Akbar (whose son's mentor, Salim Chishti, is buried at Fatehpur Sikri); the Tijaniyya, founded by Ahmad al-Tijani; the Senussi, descended from Idris I; and West African orders discussed below. The tradition is best known in the West through the Mevlevi order, founded by followers of the 13th-century Persian poet Rumi (author of the Masnavi, or "Spiritual Couplets," and the Divan dedicated to his teacher Shams-i Tabrizi) and famous for the "whirling dervishes" of the sama (or sema) ceremony, in which practitioners spin — a practice inspired by planetary motion and considered a form of dhikr — during a ceremony section called the Four Salams, accompanied by the ney flute and taksim improvisation, seeking the ecstatic state of wajd. A parallel devotional-music tradition, qawwali, traces to the Chishti order and was carried to global audiences by Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan and the Sabri Brothers, with Nusrat's crossover album Mustt Mustt (with Michael Brook), his work on Peter Gabriel's Last Temptation of Christ soundtrack, and a Massive Attack remix — the first Urdu song to chart in the UK. Central to all Sufi practice is dhikr, ritualized remembrance through recitation of God's names (sometimes with prayer beads or in a silent Naqshbandi form), aimed at achieving fana (ego-annihilation, "dying before one dies"), followed by baqa ("perpetual existence") and ultimately tawhid, divine unity. Foundational Sufi thought was shaped by the 13th-century Andalusian mystic Ibn Arabi (called ash-Shaykh al-Akbar, "the Greatest Master"), author of The Meccan Openings and The Ringstones of Wisdom, who developed the concepts of al-insan al-kamil ("the Perfect Man") and wahdat al-wujud ("unity of being"); and by the theologian al-Ghazali, whose Revival of the Religious Sciences reconciled Sufism with mainstream Sunni orthodoxy and whose Incoherence of the Philosophers devastated the rationalist falsafa tradition of al-Farabi, Avicenna, and Averroes. In West Africa, the Qadiriyya-affiliated Sokoto Caliphate, founded by Usman dan Fodio, and the Senegalese Muridiyya ("Mouride") brotherhood, founded by Amadou Bamba and centered on the holy city of Touba, are especially prominent; Usman dan Fodio's daughter, the scholar-poet Nana Asma'u, founded the Yan Taru movement for women's Islamic education. Sufi devotional life centers heavily on venerating saints at shrine-tombs — dargahs in South Asia, marabout tombs in West/North Africa — practices for which Salafi and Wahhabi critics accuse Sufis of shirk (idolatry) or bid'ah (unlawful innovation), the latter charge also leveled at the widely Sufi-popularized Mawlid an-Nabi holiday commemorating Muhammad's birth.
recursive_suggestions: Rumi — dense enough (Masnavi, Shams-i-Tabrizi, Divan, Coleman Barks controversy) to merit its own Literature or Religion deep dive; watch overlap with this page's Figure section
Ibn Arabi — could deserve a standalone Philosophy or Religion page given his metaphysics (wahdat al-wujud, insan al-kamil, barzakh); flag for Philosophy category cross-reference
Al-Ghazali — recurring figure across Religion and Philosophy topics (kalam, falsafa debates); cross-link if a standalone al-Ghazali topic exists
Sokoto Caliphate — appeared as its own bonus part answerline (History category) with Nana Asma'u/Usman dan Fodio; recommend Religion or History category filter
Islam — general/parent topic; many clues here prompt on "Islam" before the Sufism-specific giveaway, so cross-link tightly
Yazidism — appeared tangentially in a Kurdish-religions bonus alongside Sufism but is a distinct religion; do not conflate
